perturbation.original_text: 6. RECORD INSPECTION, AUDIT AND INCONTESTABILITY PERIOD A. COMPANY will maintain accurate books and records with respect to the calculation of all payments due under this Agreement. The Licensors shall have the right, upon reasonable notice, to inspect COMPANY'S books and records and all other documents and material in COMPANY'S possession or control with respect to the Content each has or might license to CONTENT PROVIDER which becomes the subject matter of this Agreement (and only with respect to Content each has or might license to CONTENT PROVIDER which become the subject matter hereof). B. All books and records relative to COMPANY'S obligations to a particular Licensor hereunder shall be maintained and made accessible to that Licensor for inspection at a location in Los Angeles, California for at least twelve (12) months after termination of this Agreement. C. Each report rendered by COMPANY to a Licensor hereunder shall become final and incontestable twelve (12) months following the date COMPANY might render same to that Licensor unless prior to the expiration of that twelve (12) month period that Licensor provides COMPANY with a detailed written objection thereto.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. RECORD INSPECTION, AUDIT AND INCONTESTABILITY PERIOD A. COMPANY will maintain books and records with respect to the calculation of payments under this Agreement. The Licensors may inspect COMPANY'S books and records with respect to the Content. B. Books and records relative to COMPANY'S obligations to a particular Licensor hereunder shall be maintained and made accessible to that Licensor for inspection at a location determined by COMPANY for twelve (12) months. C. Each report rendered by COMPANY to a Licensor hereunder shall become final and incontestable following the date COMPANY might render same unless that Licensor provides COMPANY with a written objection thereto.
perturbation.explanation: This change significantly weakens the licensor's audit rights by removing the requirements for 'accurate' records, 'reasonable notice', the full scope of documents they are entitled to view, and the provision that the inspection take place in Los Angeles. It also removes the timeframe for objections to reports rendered by company, allowing the company to not address concerns raised by the licensor if not filed in a timeframe as previously set by the contract.
perturbation.law_explanation: CCP § 1286.6 details the court's ability to correct arbitration awards under certain conditions. By weakening audit rights and dispute resolution processes, the contract could be interpreted to obstruct a party's ability to gather evidence necessary to challenge an award based on factual errors or misconduct, which could fall under grounds for correction as described in this statute, thereby potentially conflicting with or undermining the statute's intent.

OF SPECIAL PROCEEDINGS OF A CIVIL NATURE [1063 - 1822.60] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 9. ARBITRATION [1280 - 1294.4] ( Title 9 repealed and added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461. ) CHAPTER 4. Enforcement of the Award [1285 - 1288.8] ( Chapter 4 added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461. ) ARTICLE 1. Confirmation, Correction or Vacation of the Award [1285 - 1287.6] ( Article 1 added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461. ) 1286.6. Subject to Section 1286.8, the court, unless it vacates the award pursuant to Section 1286.2, shall correct the award and confirm it as corrected if the court determines that: (a) There was an evident miscalculation of figures or an evident mistake in the description of any person, thing or property referred to in the award; (b) The arbitrators exceeded their powers but the award may be corrected without affecting the merits of the decision upon the controversy submitted; or (c) The award is imperfect in a matter of form, not affecting the merits of the controversy. (Added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461.)
perturbation.justification: The changed contract text weakens the licensor's audit rights, which could obstruct their ability to challenge an arbitration award based on factual errors. CCP § 1286.6 outlines the court's ability to correct arbitration awards. The original contract ensured 'accurate books', 'reasonable notice', a defined scope of accessible documents, and a set objection timeframe. By removing these elements, the modified contract impairs the licensor's ability to gather evidence needed to potentially challenge an award, thereby creating a contradiction with the intent of CCP § 1286.6.

perturbation.original_text: 15. GOVERNING LAW AND VENUE This Agreement is to be governed by and construed in accordance with the Laws of the State of California applicable to contracts made and to be performed wholly within such State, and without regard to the conflicts of laws principles thereof. Any suit brought hereon, whether in contract, tort, equity or otherwise, shall be brought in the state or federal courts sitting in Los Angeles County, California, the parties hereto hereby waiving any claim or defense that such forum is not convenient or proper. Each party hereby agrees that any such court shall have in personam jurisdiction over it, consents to service of process in any manner prescribed or authorized by California Law, and agrees that a final judgment in any such action or proceeding shall be conclusive and may be enforced in other jurisdictions by suit on the judgment or in any other manner specified by Law.
perturbation.changed_text: 15. GOVERNING LAW AND VENUE This Agreement is to be governed by and construed in accordance with the Laws of a jurisdiction chosen by Company. Any suit brought hereon, whether in contract, tort, equity or otherwise, shall be brought in a court chosen by the company, the parties hereto waiving any claim or defense that such forum is not convenient or proper. Each party hereby agrees that any such court shall have in personam jurisdiction over it and agrees that a judgment in any such action or proceeding shall be conclusive.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the explicit reference to California law and Los Angeles County as the venue, and giving the Company sole discretion to choose both, this clause now introduces a potential legal contradiction. It allows the company to choose a jurisdiction with laws less favorable to the content provider or a venue that is unreasonably difficult for the content provider to access, essentially waiving rights to a fair legal process.
perturbation.law_explanation: CCP § 410.30 allows a California Court the power to stay or dismiss an action if the court finds that in the interest of substantial justice the action should be heard in a forum outside California. By giving the Company sole power to choose jurisdiction and venue, it could be argued that the contract is attempting to contractually restrict the court's ability to apply 410.30. Further, a California court will scrutinize contracts that effectively prevent a resident party from seeking remedies, potentially rendering this provision unenforceable under California public policy.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 410.30 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. JURISDICTION AND SERVICE OF PROCESS [410.10 - 418.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1969, Ch. 1610. ) CHAPTER 1. Jurisdiction and Forum [410.10 - 410.70] ( Heading of Chapter 1 amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 615, Sec. 3. ) ARTICLE 2. Forum [410.30 - 410.42] ( Heading of Article 2 amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 615, Sec. 4. ) 410.30. (a) When a court upon motion of a party or its own motion finds that in the interest of substantial justice an action should be heard in a forum outside this state, the court shall stay or dismiss the action in whole or in part on any conditions that may be just. (b) The provisions of Section 418.10 do not apply to a motion to stay or dismiss the action by a defendant who has made a general appearance. (Amended by Stats. 1972, Ch. 601. Note: This version was suspended from Sept. 22, 1986, until Jan. 1, 1992, during operation of the temporary amendment by Stats. 1986, Ch. 968.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the explicit application of California law and Los Angeles County as the venue, granting the Company the unilateral right to choose the governing jurisdiction and venue. This change contradicts CCP § 410.30, which allows a California court to stay or dismiss an action if it determines that, in the interest of substantial justice, the case should be heard outside California. By giving the Company sole power over jurisdiction and venue, the contract potentially restricts the court's ability to apply CCP § 410.30. The original text ensured disputes were resolved within California's legal framework and a specific venue. The changed text eliminates this protection, creating a contradiction by allowing the company to choose a jurisdiction potentially unfavorable or inaccessible to the content provider, thus undermining the principles of fair legal process and potentially restricting access to remedies within California.

perturbation.original_text: 16. ARBITRATION Any controversy or claim arising out of or relating to this Agreement, or any agreements or instruments relating hereto or delivered in connection herewith or the breach, termination, enforcement, interpretation or validity thereof, including the determination of the scope or applicablility of this agreement to arbitrate, will at the request of any party be determined by arbitration in Los Angeles, California before three (3) arbitrators under the rules of the JAMS. Judgment upon the award rendered by the arbitrators may be entered in any court having jurisdiction. The institution and maintenance of an action for judicial relief in pursuit of a provisional or ancillary remedy shall not constitute a waiver of the right of any party, including the plaintiff, to submit the controversy or claim to arbitration.
perturbation.changed_text: 16. ARBITRATION Any controversy or claim arising out of or relating to this Agreement will be determined by arbitration under rules chosen by the Company. Judgment upon the award rendered by the arbitrators may be entered in any court. The institution and maintenance of an action for judicial relief in pursuit of a remedy shall constitute a waiver of the right of any party to submit the controversy or claim to arbitration.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the specification of 'Los Angeles, California', 'three arbitrators', 'under the rules of JAMS', and by stating the pursuit of ANY remedy waives the right to arbitration, this clause creates a contradiction. It removes consumer protections inherent in the JAMS rules (or similar arbitration services) and makes access to remedies unfairly restricted. The company has free reign to choose arbiters.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 1281.9 mandates specific disclosures by potential arbitrators, including any relationships or prior dealings that might create a conflict of interest. It also allows parties to disqualify arbitrators under certain circumstances. By omitting any mention of rules or procedures governing arbitrator selection and disclosure, the modified clause effectively allows the company to select arbitrators without these legally mandated safeguards, potentially biasing the arbitration process and contradicting the intent of the statute. Removing reference to JAMS also removes some measure of protection for fair arbitration.

OF SPECIAL PROCEEDINGS OF A CIVIL NATURE [1063 - 1822.60] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 9. ARBITRATION [1280 - 1294.4] ( Title 9 repealed and added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461. ) CHAPTER 2. Enforcement of Arbitration Agreements [1281 - 1281.99] ( Chapter 2 added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461. ) 1281.9. (a) In any arbitration pursuant to an arbitration agreement, when a person is to serve as a neutral arbitrator, the proposed neutral arbitrator shall disclose all matters that could cause a person aware of the facts to reasonably entertain a doubt that the proposed neutral arbitrator would be able to be impartial, including all of the following: (1) The existence of any ground specified in Section 170.1 for disqualification of a judge. For purposes of paragraph (8) of subdivision (a) of Section 170.1, the proposed neutral arbitrator shall disclose whether or not they have a current arrangement concerning prospective employment or other compensated service as a dispute resolution neutral or are participating in, or, within the last two years, have participated in, discussions regarding such prospective employment or service with a party to the proceeding. (2) Any matters required to be disclosed by the ethics standards for neutral arbitrato
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts California Code of Civil Procedure § 1281.9, specifically regarding the disclosure requirements for neutral arbitrators. By omitting the specification of arbitration rules (JAMS) and procedures for selecting arbitrators, the modified clause allows the company to select arbitrators without the legally mandated safeguards, violating the intent of CCP 1281.9. Snippet 1 explicitly states that a proposed neutral arbitrator 'shall disclose all matters that could cause a person aware of the facts to reasonably entertain a doubt that the proposed neutral arbitrator would be able to be impartial,' including grounds for disqualification. The removal of JAMS or similar standards removes these protections.
